title: First International Conference on Fossil Fuel Transition Opens in Santa Marta
detail: The First Conference on Transitioning Away from Fossil Fuels formally opened in Santa Marta, Colombia on April 24, 2026 — the world's first international summit dedicated exclusively to fossil fuel phase-out pathways. Co-hosted by Colombia and the Netherlands with backing from Brazil's COP30 Presidency, the summit brought together 54 nations and the European Union for academic forums, a People's Summit, and high-level government talks (April 28-29). The conference aims to develop country- and sector-specific phase-out roadmaps, operating outside of UNFCCC's all-inclusive but slow-moving consensus framework. A 12-point 'Santa Marta Synthesis' report from a top scientists' group was commissioned as policy input. Major fossil fuel producers including Norway, Australia, and Mexico attended alongside Pacific island states. The US, China, Russia, India, and Saudi Arabia were notably absent.

media.caption: The First Conference on Transitioning Away from Fossil Fuels opens in Santa Marta, Colombia with 54+ nations

title: Science Panel for Global Energy Transition (SPGET) Launched at Santa Marta
detail: The Science Panel for the Global Energy Transition (SPGET) was officially launched on April 24, 2026 in Santa Marta, convened by renowned climate scientists Johan Rockström (Stockholm Resilience Centre) and Carlos Nobre (University of São Paulo), with backing from Brazil's COP30 Presidency. SPGET will develop country- and sector-level emissions milestones aligned with 1.5°C pathways and provide evidence-based guidance for fossil fuel phase-out policies — functioning as an independent scientific advisory body to the growing coalition of nations committed to transitioning away from oil, gas, and coal. A draft fossil fuel phase-out roadmap for Colombia was also unveiled at the launch event as a model for other fossil fuel-producing nations.

media.caption: Science Panel for Global Energy Transition (SPGET) launched by Johan Rockström and Carlos Nobre in Santa Marta
